name: Prague
summary: Prague is consistently ranked among Europe's safest capitals, with very low violent crime rates and excellent safety infrastructure. The main concerns for tourists are petty theft, pickpocketing in crowded areas, and occasional tourist-targeted scams.
quickVerdict: Prague is exceptionally safe for tourists, with official Level 1 travel advisory from the U.S. State Department. While violent crime is extremely rare, visitors should remain vigilant against pickpockets in tourist hotspots and be aware of common scams involving taxi drivers, currency exchange, and overcharging at restaurants. The city's excellent public transport system and strong police presence make it ideal for all types of travelers, including solo women.

neighborhoods.name: Old Town (Staré Město)
neighborhoods.description: The historic heart of Prague with major attractions like the Astronomical Clock and Charles Bridge. Well-lit and heavily patrolled but watch for pickpockets in crowds.

neighborhoods.name: Lesser Town (Malá Strana)
neighborhoods.description: Charming cobblestone district below Prague Castle. Very safe with good lighting and regular police patrols, though some quiet streets can feel isolated at night.

neighborhoods.name: New Town (Nové Město)
neighborhoods.description: Modern city center including Wenceslas Square. Generally safe but avoid excessive drinking areas around Dlouha Street late at night.

neighborhoods.name: Vinohrady
neighborhoods.description: Upscale residential area popular with expats. Very safe neighborhood with good restaurants and cafes, well-lit streets and minimal crime.

neighborhoods.name: Žižkov
neighborhoods.description: Alternative district with nightlife and the TV Tower. Generally safe but can feel grittier, especially around the train station area late at night.

neighborhoods.name: Smíchov
neighborhoods.description: Developing area with shopping centers and nightlife. Safe during the day but exercise caution in quieter areas after dark, particularly around Anděl metro station.

scams.name: Taxi Overcharging
scams.description: Dishonest taxi drivers manipulate meters, quote inflated flat rates, or take unnecessarily long routes, especially from the airport and train stations.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ride-sharing apps like Uber or Bolt which provide transparent pricing. If using traditional taxis, always agree on price beforehand and ensure the meter is running.

scams.name: Currency Exchange Scam
scams.description: Exchange offices in tourist areas advertise good rates but have hidden fees or minimum exchange amounts in fine print. Street exchangers may offer counterfeit money.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official bank ATMs or reputable exchange offices. Always read the fine print and calculate the total amount you'll receive before handing over money.

scams.name: Restaurant Overcharging
scams.description: Some tourist-area restaurants add unauthorized charges for bread, service fees, or switch menus to show higher prices when the bill arrives.
scams.howToAvoid: Check bills carefully and ask about any charges you don't recognize. Avoid restaurants that don't display prices clearly or seem overly eager to seat tourists.

scams.name: Fake Charity Petitions
scams.description: Scammers approach tourists with clipboards asking them to sign petitions for fake charities, then demand donations or use the distraction for pickpocketing.
scams.howToAvoid: Politely decline to sign any petitions from strangers. Keep walking and don't engage. Public fundraising has been banned in Prague's city center since March 2025.

soloFemale.overview: Prague is excellent for solo female travelers, ranking as the 80th safest place globally for women traveling alone with a 4.5/5 safety rating. The city has well-lit streets, reliable public transport, and low rates of harassment. Women report feeling 91% safe during the day and 73% safe at night.
tips: Stay in well-lit, populated areas especially after dark
Use official transport options like Uber, Bolt, or public transit rather than street taxis
Keep valuables secure and be extra vigilant in crowded tourist areas
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from any uncomfortable situations
Consider joining organized tours or staying in social accommodation to meet other travelers

nightSafety.overview: Prague is generally safe at night with good lighting in central areas and regular police patrols. The city center remains bustling after dark, and public transport operates reliably. However, avoid poorly lit side streets, parks, and areas around some metro stations late at night.
tips: Stick to main streets and well-lit areas, especially in the city center
Avoid parks, quiet alleys, and areas around Anděl or Florenc stations very late
Use night trams and buses which run every 30 minutes after midnight
Be extra cautious on weekends when drunk crowds may be more prevalent
Consider taking rideshare services for door-to-door transport late at night

transport.metro: Prague's metro system is safe, efficient, and operates from 5:00-24:00 with three color-coded lines. Stations are well-maintained with good security but watch for pickpockets during crowded times. Night service is replaced by trams and buses.
transport.rideshare: Uber and Bolt are widely available and considered the safest taxi option with transparent pricing and GPS tracking. Both apps work reliably throughout the city and are significantly safer than street taxis.
transport.taxis: Traditional street taxis have a poor reputation for overcharging tourists. Official airport taxis and hotel-arranged taxis are generally reliable. Always agree on price beforehand or ensure the meter is running.
transport.tips: Public transport is excellent and very safe - get a daily pass for convenience. Validate paper tickets in yellow machines or face heavy fines from plainclothes inspectors.

customs: Greet with 'Dobrý den' (Good day) and a firm handshake with eye contact - Czechs appreciate this basic courtesy
Remove shoes when entering someone's home - this is standard practice in Czech households
Maintain punctuality for appointments - being late is considered disrespectful in Czech culture
Keep conversations at moderate volume in public spaces - Czechs value calm, quiet behavior on transport and in public
Wait for everyone to be served before eating and finish everything on your plate to show appreciation

health.overview: Czech Republic has excellent healthcare ranked 8th globally by the World Index of Healthcare Innovation. Prague has modern hospitals with English-speaking staff and high-quality medical care.
health.water: Tap water is safe to drink throughout Prague and meets high EU standards. It's closely monitored and regulated, though some visitors may prefer bottled water.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required for Prague. Routine vaccinations like MMR should be up to date. EU health insurance cards accepted for EU citizens.
health.altitude: Prague sits at low elevation with a temperate continental climate. Summers can be hot (up to 25°C) while winters are cold with snow. No altitude-related health concerns.

emergency.usEmbassy: U.S. Embassy Prague: +420-257-022-000 (24/7), located at Tržiště 15, 118 01 Praha 1. For emergencies after hours call the same number.

faq.a: Yes, Prague is very safe for tourists and consistently ranks among Europe's safest capitals. The U.S. State Department gives it a Level 1 (Exercise Normal Precautions) rating. Violent crime is extremely rare, with the main concerns being petty theft and tourist scams.

Prague is generally safe at night, especially in the city center which remains well-lit and populated. Stick to main streets and avoid parks, quiet alleys, and some metro station areas very late at night. Night public transport runs regularly and safely.

faq.q: Is Prague safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Yes, Prague is excellent for solo female travelers, ranking 80th globally for women's safety. Women report feeling 91% safe during the day and 73% safe at night. Harassment is uncommon, and the city has good infrastructure and reliable transport.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Prague?
faq.a: While Prague is generally safe everywhere, exercise extra caution around Anděl and Florenc metro stations late at night, avoid parks after dark, and be vigilant in crowded tourist areas like Old Town Square where pickpockets operate.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Prague?
faq.a: Yes, Prague's public transport is very safe and efficient. The metro, trams, and buses are well-maintained with good security. Watch for pickpockets during crowded times and always validate your tickets to avoid fines from inspectors.
